text: Governor Pritzker says Mayor Johnson's Bears pitch is a non-starter. I'd love them to be in the city, but we're three years in now and he still has no plan. And I'll talk with Crain's commercial real estate reporter, Rachel Herzog, about suburban apartment property news. I would say the Chicago suburbs probably don't have the, you know, big highs and lows that other markets like the Sunbelt do. You know, they've seen rent growth, but it's been kind of moderate. I'm Amy Guth, and this is Crain's Daily Gist for Tuesday.
Today, May 19th.
Because when you succeed, they succeed. Old National Bank, where relationships and results meet. Connect with a banker today at oldnational.com slash commercial banking. Member FDIC.

I'm joined by Crain's commercial real estate reporter, Rachel Herzog, here to talk about apartments. Lots of apartment things to talk about. So welcome back to the podcast, Rachel. Hi, Amy. Thanks so much for having me. Of course. So let's start by talking about this O'Hare area complex. It sold for $167 million, and that is among the priciest apartment deals this year, but there is a nuance to it. Tell me about that.

Yeah, so the news today is that Chicago-based real estate firm RIG Capital paid $167 million for the Pavilion Apartments. It's on Chicago's far northwest side, just within city limits, across the street from Rosemont. So, you know, it's basically comparable to other suburban apartment complexes, more of a similar property type to those, versus downtown high-rises.

Word in the suburbs, it would be a record for the priciest suburban apartment deal, but it's in city limits, so I can't technically call it that. But it is the priciest local apartment deal or the most paid for a single apartment complex so far in 2026. So we can say that. And it's up against that record, though, right? Chicago's is $175 million for a tower.

So that's the priciest, I think, in two or three years, but I think the record is actually over $200 million, but it's been a few years since that. Okay, so that's a recent record. Then you noted in reporting that the suburban record is about $148 million. So that's, it's an interesting building location-wise. It's kind of in between city and suburbs, but also price-wise, right? It kind of falls in that zone. Right, right. You could say it's a suburban asset with a Chicago address.

I like that. That's a much easier way to say that than what I did. All right. Tell me about the property and its purchase. Right. So this is one of the largest properties in the area. It's 1,115 units. It's kind of what's known as naturally affordable or workforce housing, meaning the rents fall. This is not a luxury building. The rents fall a little bit.

below, I think, the median net rent for the suburbs. So they're not subsidized, but a little bit more within reach for people. So it went up for sale back in 2023. The seller is Brookfield Asset Management, you know, real estate giant. And they acquired it back in 2018 through a $6.8 billion acquisition of another realty trust. Right. And so they assumed the whole portfolio and that was part of it.
Right, right. So we don't have their individual part of the thing or what they paid for it in that deal. What does median net rent look like in the suburbs right now?

Yeah, so the suburbs have seen pretty steady rent growth. The median net rent in the fourth quarter of 2025 was up 2.6% year over year. According to Integra Realty Resources, it rose to $2.15 per square foot. So that's kind of in line with what we've seen with suburban rent growth the past few years. You know, not as much as downtown rents. I think the suburbs are seeing a little more development, but it is still kind of constrained due to kind of financing and broader factors. So definitely a

supply and demand dynamic that appeals to investors. Yeah, definitely. Well, in other apartment news, talk to me about the Chicago firm that has paid $91 million for Cain County Apartments. Right. So we had news about another high dollar deal yesterday. Insonia Properties, which I believe is a local firm, paid about $91 million for the 352-unit villages at Canterfield. So that's in the far northwest suburbs in West Dundee.

Definitely among the first major suburban rental property sales of 2026, we saw a $102 million sale out of Orland Ridge back in March, and then there was the $110 million sale of a complex in Crest Hill in the southwest suburbs in January. So we've seen a few of these sales. And how do these compare to the last property that we were talking about that you described as workforce housing?

Yeah, based on my understanding, this is kind of a similar type of asset known as workforce housing. And Sonia Properties said on LinkedIn that, you know, their investment strategy properties they buy includes, quote, deepening affordability for working families. So the rents at this complex are a little bit below the median net rent in the overall Chicago suburbs, as we talked about. They also said in their LinkedIn post that they plan to, quote, enhance operations through targeted capital improvements. So that indicates that they're probably planning.
Yeah, definitely. And then let's go to Elmhurst. Talk to me about these apartments that have sold to an Ohio pension fund for $85 million.

Right, so the State Teachers Retirement System of Ohio paid $85 million for an apartment property known as The Finn. It's in downtown Elmhurst. But what's actually interesting about this deal is the seller. They're an apartment development firm known as Cartera, which is part of national home builder, Lenar. And Cartera's kind of been, you know, reevaluating their apartment portfolio. They've listed and sold quite a few apartment buildings nationally.

Particular sale comes a few months after global alternative asset management firm TPG acquired a majority stake in Cortera, but TPG was not a part of this deal. This deal was in the works before then, I believe. And you've put some price points in the reporting here, and these seem like they're at the much higher end of suburban apartments. Yeah, this is not what we would consider workforce housing. I think, you know, rents at this complex range from about...

Just shy of $2,000 for a studio apartment, which is kind of comparable to what you might see downtown. So these are in downtown Elmhurst near the Metro Station, you know, very desirable area. This is, you know, a top of the line apartment building with, you know, Class A, all the amenities. Yeah. Well, it's been about a month or so since we've talked about apartments, but, you know, focusing in on suburban apartments, how would you, how would you characterize how that market is looking right now? Or maybe just even the Chicago area overall, not really?

You know, just focusing on suburbs. Yeah, I would say the Chicago suburbs probably don't have the, you know, big highs and lows that other markets like the Sunbelt do. You know, they've seen rent growth, but it's been, you know, kind of moderate. But I think that's probably something that, you know, appeals to certain types of investors that just want something that's, you know, they know there's going to be steady demand for these properties.

In conversations about the city, we've talked about just the lack of new apartments being built. Are we seeing that same kind of constraint in the suburbs? Yeah, not quite as much constraint. I think there probably has been, you know, moderate amount of development across the suburbs. But yeah, there is some, you know, I would say it's limited new supply hitting the market in the suburbs. And another factor boosting demand that I didn't get into is, you know, people are...

still putting off home buying due to, you know, high prices, high interest rates. So they might be staying in apartments for longer and maybe paying more for rent. Right, right. Which is this crossover conversation that I have with our colleague, Dennis Rodkin, often about what's driving home buying or what's not driving home buying right now. And that's, I should have the two of you on together at the same time to just talk about the journey of housing. Indeed. All right. Well, thanks, Rachel. Always a pleasure to have you on and check in about what's going on in the market.
Thanks so much, Amy. Coming up, the closure of Spirit Airlines costs Chicago hundreds of jobs. We'll talk about that and more right after this.

This is the Crain's Daily Gist with Amy Guth.

Governor J.B. Pritzker dismissed Chicago Mayor Brandon Johnson's latest pitch to keep the Bears in the city as too little, too late, as legislators head into the last two weeks of the spring session. This is kind of typical. The mayor has shown up every spring at the end of session to pronounce what he would like to see happen. And as you know, the budget gets put together starting in November.

Governor Pritzker said Monday morning at a press conference, quote, the mayor has come up with no plan how the Bears would end up in the city of Chicago, so that's problematic. Continuing by saying, quote, I would love for them to be in the city.

He also said, quote, we're three years in and he still has no plan. The Bears have said publicly they have only two options, the state of Indiana or Arlington Heights. We would like them to stay in the state of Illinois, so I'm fighting hard to make sure they can do that. John Pletz noted in reporting that the Bears saga is just the latest example of how the governor and the mayor frequently have differing agendas. Johnson, two years ago, proposed building a new lakefront stadium, getting the support of
Thank you for watching, and I'll see you next time.

The mayor's idea for ISFA would allow the city to control how future money is allocated while potentially expanding the agency's authority to finance other tourism-related infrastructure projects. The mayor insists the deal he proposed with the Bears two years ago remained the best option for the team and for taxpayers because the Chicago Park District would own the stadium and receive a cut of the revenue it produces. The mayor was in Springfield a couple of weeks ago to make a pitch for Chicago.

and other municipalities to get more of state income tax funding that's shared with cities and towns and maintain his support for keeping the NFL team in the city as legislators continue a new law that would allow the Bears and others to lock in their property tax payments over decades. But Governor Pritzker said the mayor has not discussed the ISFA idea with him.

Pletz noted in reporting that the Illinois General Assembly is still weighing broader legislation that would allow the Bears to negotiate with local taxing authorities to significantly reduce their property tax bill by instead agreeing to a payment in lieu of taxes, or pilot. After months of wrangling, the House passed a bill that included provisions for property tax relief and an amusement tax that are still proving problematic. To win support of the bill, the House included a provision to set aside 50% of the pilot for local and

and statewide property tax relief. The Senate has yet to take up the bill. Governor Pritzker said of the proposal to keep the team, quote, that's in the legislature's hands. I put the structure of a deal together with the bears. The Senate has work to do. They're going to make changes to the bill, no doubt. I would expect we would see something before May 31st and both houses would vote on that. Pletz noted that Indiana lawmakers passed legislation that enables the creation of a sports authority in Northwest Indiana.

that would fund a new stadium in Hammond. But the local legislation in two counties that would create hotel, food, beverage, and other taxes to fund the project has not been proposed or passed. The Chicago Bears have said they plan to make a decision on where to build the stadium by late spring or early summer.

The University of Chicago, known for tough academic standards, is no longer getting graded on a curve when it comes to the school's balance sheet. Crain's John Pletz reported that Fitch Ratings downgraded UChicago one notch to a AA rating with a stable outlook from AA+, largely based on its debt-to-liquid assets ratio, even though the university has run an operating deficit in recent years. A senior director at Fitch

The New York-based credit rating agency said, quote, the University of Chicago has a lot of considerable strengths. Continuing, quote, this is among the most selective institutions, and it's seen noticeable growth in applications for a decade. That trend continues. He also said, quote, the downgrade spoke to the fact that the university's available funds relative to its debt just doesn't stack up to what our expectation is for a AA-rated private university.

Pletz noted that a surge in construction and other capital spending a decade ago has left the university with about $5 billion in debt. It has unrestricted cash and other liquid assets worth $7 billion, according to Fitch. And it's the first credit downgrade in recent memory for UChicago. But even after the Fitch downgrade, the university remains just two notches below the top rating available of AAA.
President Paula Levisados, who took over in 2021, has been working to shore up the school's finances. Its string of operating deficits peaked at $288 million in 2024, leading to layoffs last year.

The university said, quote, as reflected in the Fitch report, the University of Chicago has reported substantial and continuing progress on its budget deficit, including a reduction of $128 million in fiscal year 25 and a further reduction of approximately $30 million anticipated in fiscal year 2026, advancing the university's multi-year plan to eliminate its operating budget deficit. Let's note that the university said it expects to be back in the black by the end of the decade.

Fitch also said, noting that enrollment has grown more than 20% since 2017 to 18,400 students, quote, revenue sources are well diversified, supported in part by strong fundraising and UChicago's significant healthcare presence.

The dilapidated main building of the Ford City Mall on the southwest side is closing next month because of the property's hazardous conditions, while other parts of the troubled shopping center can stay open for now. Crain's Danny Ecker reported that under an agreement announced on Friday between the City of Chicago and the owner of the mall at 7601 West Cicero, the 66-acre property's main building will be closed to the general public as of noon on June 22.
lawsuit against mall owner Namdar Realty Group for allegedly violating the city's building code by failing to fix the faulty fire suppression system

As previously discussed here on the podcast, city officials recently highlighted issues like sinkholes and other deterioration as what was described as an imminent threat to tenants and shoppers at the property. A total of 24 stores at the main mall will be forced to close, including anchor tenant JCPenney, according to a copy of the order. And while the agreement partially resolves the city's push, other properties on the site served by the fire suppression system, including a freestanding retail building,

are not subject to the order and can remain open. City officials confirmed on Friday during a virtual court hearing that the separated northern portion of the mall has a functioning sprinkler system. Another hearing on the case is scheduled for June 29th.

And as Ecker noted, the agreement also leaves an open question about the future of the $150 million plan from Chicago-based Curve Industrial to redevelop the main mall site with four warehouses. Curve is under contract to buy the main building from Great Neck, New York-based Namdar, though the developer has yet to win approval from the city council to rezone the mall for the project.

Sources familiar with the situation told Crain's that Curve's completion of the entitlement process and acquisition of the property hinge on an adequate resolution of the safety litigation. And it's unclear whether the main building's shutdown will be sufficient for that project to move forward, and a spokesperson for Curve did not immediately respond to Crain's request for comment.

As Ecker previously reported, Namdar Realty sought to keep the mall open after a successful test last month of the more than 960,000 square foot building's fire suppression system, which provides water pressure to fight fires at a range of buildings on the property. But city officials said that fix was temporary and stressed concerns over the structural stability of the mall. Chicago Fire Department Lieutenant Robert Steffens, a longtime court section supervisor who has worked with the city and Namdar to

address the problem, reiterated the existing safety risk during a court hearing last month. In addition to spotlighting the non-functioning suppression system and fire alarm system, Steffens also estimated tens of thousands of gallons per day have leaked for the past two years into unknown areas of the ground and potentially contributed to sinkholes forming around sewer basins on the property.

And as Ecker also reported, another big stakeholder in the safety issue is confectionery company Tootsie Roll Industries, whose massive headquarters and manufacturing plant is adjacent to the northern portion of the mall that is not subject to the order. Tootsie Roll attorney Tom Raines last month warned a Cook County judge that any sale of the main mall building would not automatically eliminate the safety problems for other properties.

Chicago Assistant Corporation Counsel Greg Janes said during Friday's hearing that other freestanding retail buildings and northern mall building tenants could still be affected as the case progresses. More than 350 workers in Chicago lost their jobs when Spirit Airlines closed down earlier this month.

Crain's John Pletz reported that most of the local job cuts involved flight crews, including 100 pilots and 239 flight attendants. That according to a new filing with the state. Pletz noted that Chicago had become a relatively small market for the Florida-based discount carrier, but it's a major hub for pilots and other aviation workers because of the presence of United American and Southwest at O'Hare and Midway airports. A decade ago,

Florida-based Spirit was O'Hare's third largest carrier by passenger volume. Before it ceased operations, the airline had fallen into fifth place, operating at about 30 flights a day from O'Hare to 14 cities. And as Pletz also noted, before the pandemic, which reset the air travel market, Spirit was one of the fastest growing carriers at O'Hare.

The airline twice declared bankruptcy before ceasing operations on May 2nd. It's the first U.S. casualty in a post-pandemic shakeout of the airline industry. And as Pletz noted in reporting, rising labor costs and lack of availability of new aircraft, which kept a lid on the volume of new cheap flights, crimped the discount airline's business model and leveled the playing field for traditional full-service carriers like United and American in the competition for leisure travelers.

And meanwhile, traditional full-service carriers used no-frills basic economy tickets subsidized by lucrative credit card partnerships and increased full-fare premium demand coming out of the pandemic to win back market share from the discount airlines.

That's Crain's Daily Gist for now. Check in on our continuous news feed at chicagobusiness.com. Thanks so much to today's guest, Crain's commercial real estate reporter, Rachel Herzog. You can follow all of our conversations on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, or wherever you like to get your audio on demand. Don't forget to subscribe and please rate and review Crain's Daily Gist. Our show is produced by Todd Manley at Earsight Studios.
Thanks so much for listening. I'm Amy Guth, and I'll meet you right back here next time.
